root_motivation: LLMs should never perform arithmetic on result rows; returning JSON and asking the model to 'sum column X' is a stochastic correctness problem that silently drifts on multi-step operations. Moving all post-query operations server-side into a deterministic, unit-tested Rust kernel turns a correctness gamble into a guarantee — the same input always yields byte-identical output, and the guarantee is enforced by tests, not inference.
user_persona: An MCP server (dh-mcp-server) calling dh-ops on behalf of an LLM client. The LLM picks which operation and parameters; dh-ops does all arithmetic server-side and returns only a new DatasetHandle + summary, never raw rows.

acceptance_criteria.description: aggregate sum/mean/min/max/count/count_distinct each verified against hand-computed golden over a fixture dataset (one test per agg function).
acceptance_criteria.test: tests::ac1_aggregate_sum, ac1_aggregate_mean, ac1_aggregate_min, ac1_aggregate_max, ac1_aggregate_count, ac1_aggregate_count_distinct

acceptance_criteria.description: filter with a compound AND/OR predicate returns exactly the expected row subset; numeric and string predicates both covered.
sort is stable and correct for multi-key asc/desc; top_n returns the right N and breaks ties deterministically (documented tie-break rule: smaller original row index wins).
pivot produces the correct crosstab for a 2-dimension × 1-measure fixture.
compare over two handles yields correct delta + pct-change and records a 2-parent lineage.
drill expands a grouped row to its detail rows by walking lineage to the parent.
Every op returns a NEW handle (never mutates input) and an OpResult whose summary contains no more than sample_cap rows.
Determinism: running any op twice on the same input yields byte-identical stored output.
cargo test --release passes; cargo clippy --release -- -D warnings clean.

scope: Implement aggregate, filter, sort, top_n, pivot, compare, drill, describe as pure fn (Dataset, params) -> Dataset operations
Each op calls store.derive(...) to allocate a new handle; no op mutates input or returns raw rows
Operations must be deterministic: same input + same params => byte-identical output
Depend on dh-spec (DatasetHandle, ColumnSchema, DType, Capability, OpResult), dh-store (Store, Dataset, ColumnData), dh-summary (summarize, SummaryCfg)
Expose public API: one function per operation, all signature fn op(store: &mut Store, handle: &DatasetHandle, params: &Value) -> Result<OpResult, OpError>
non_goals: Returning raw rows to the caller — summaries only
Query planning or SQL compilation (that is mqo-*)
Serving as an HTTP endpoint or MCP server (that is dh-mcp-server)
Schema inference or type coercion beyond what dh-spec already provides

five_whys_trace.q: Why does the dh-* MCP fleet need a separate dh-ops crate?
five_whys_trace.a: The MCP server needs to perform post-query transformations (aggregate, filter, sort, etc.) on cached dataset handles, and those operations must be deterministic and server-side.

five_whys_trace.q: Why must the operations be deterministic and server-side?
five_whys_trace.a: Asking an LLM to perform arithmetic on returned JSON rows is the LLM-as-calculator anti-pattern — the results drift silently on multi-step operations. Moving the math to Rust gives a testable guarantee.

five_whys_trace.q: Why is a Rust library the right artifact (vs inline logic in dh-mcp-server)?
five_whys_trace.a: Separating the operation kernel from the server boundary lets it be unit-tested in isolation with golden fixtures, and makes the determinism guarantees enforceable by tests, not by code review.

five_whys_trace.q: Why do all operations share the same signature fn(store, handle, params) -> Result<OpResult, OpError>?
five_whys_trace.a: A uniform signature means the MCP server can dispatch any operation through one code path, reducing dispatch logic to a single match arm per op name — no special cases per operation.

five_whys_trace.q: Why does every operation return a new handle rather than mutating the input?
five_whys_trace.a: Immutability enables the lineage graph (each handle knows its parents) which powers the drill operation — walking the graph back to the detail dataset. It also prevents cache invalidation races.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Does compare need to support multi-parent lineage in the Store API, or just encode handle_b in params?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: The current dh-store Store API is single-parent for derive(). Compare records handle_b in params (already present as 'handle_b') so the lineage is walkable via params inspection. A future multi-parent API is a non-goal for this PRD.

ambiguities_resolved.question: What is the tie-break rule for top_n?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Smaller original row index wins. This is documented in the function doc-comment and verified in AC3 test.
